pmos

Fundamentals of Marketing — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Marketing is not advertising — advertising is one small corner of it. At its root, marketing is the work of creating and exchanging things people find valuable, and the field has a small, stable set of foundational ideas every product manager should recognize. This primer maps that base layer: what marketing actually means (the American Marketing Association's definition), the idea of customer value, the STP sequence (segmentation, targeting, positioning) for deciding who you serve and how you want to be seen, the marketing mix (the famous 4 Ps) for the levers you actually pull, and the funnel models (AIDA, Kotler's 5 A's) that describe how a stranger becomes a customer. The named voices here — Philip Kotler, E. Jerome McCarthy, Al Ries and Jack Trout — disagree on emphasis but converge on one idea: marketing is the disciplined matching of an offering to the people who will value it most. Written for any PM (product manager): every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the American Marketing Association, McCarthy's and Kotler's marketing-mix canon, and Ries and Trout's positioning canon. 15 verified sources.

What marketing actually is (it is not advertising)

The most common misunderstanding about marketing is that it means advertising — the ads, the logos, the social posts. Those are real, but they are the visible tip of a much larger discipline. The American Marketing Association (AMA), the field's main professional body, defines marketing as "the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large." The key word is exchanging: marketing is fundamentally about a value exchange — you offer something a person wants, they give something (usually money, sometimes attention or data) in return.

Read that definition slowly and you will notice it covers far more than promotion. Creating the offering, communicating it, delivering it, and exchanging it are four different jobs — and advertising lives only inside "communicating." This is why marketing and product management overlap so heavily: deciding what to build, for whom, at what price, and how to get it into people's hands are marketing questions as much as product ones. Santa Clara University's business school describes the discipline's core toolkit, the 4 Ps, as having become "a staple in business schools and boardrooms alike" precisely because it organizes all of those jobs into one frame. For a PM, the useful reframe is this: if product management asks "what should we build?", marketing asks "who is this for, why will they choose it, and how will they find out?"

Customer value and the value proposition

If marketing is an exchange of value, then everything starts with understanding what the customer actually values — and being able to state it plainly. The tool for that is the value proposition (the one-sentence answer to "why should I buy this instead of the alternative, or instead of doing nothing?"). It is not a slogan and not a feature list; it is a promise about the benefit a customer will get. The encyclopedia entry on the customer value proposition defines it as "the sum total of benefits which a vendor promises a customer will receive in return for the customer's associated payment."

Two ideas hide inside that definition and both matter for PMs. First, value is defined by the customer, not the builder — a feature you are proud of creates no value if the customer does not care about it. Second, value is always relative: customers weigh your benefits against the price and against competing options, including the option of changing nothing. A strong value proposition therefore names a specific customer, a specific problem, and a specific reason you solve it better than the alternatives. Much of the rest of marketing — segmentation, targeting, positioning, the mix — exists to sharpen and deliver that one promise to the people most likely to believe it.

The STP framework: segmentation, targeting, positioning

You cannot be everything to everyone, so marketing's central strategic move is choosing who to serve and how you want them to think of you. The canonical sequence for that decision is STP, which stands for Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning. The idea traces to a 1956 paper by Wendell Smith and was popularized by Philip Kotler, often called the father of modern marketing, in his textbook Marketing Management. The encyclopedia summary describes the chain cleanly: divide the market, choose the best parts, and then differentiate for each — "Positioning proposes distinctive competitive advantages for each segment."

The three steps run in order, and each one narrows the decision the next one inherits. The diagram below shows the flow, and the three sections that follow unpack each step in turn.

Segmentation: dividing the market into groups

Segmentation is the first STP step: breaking a large, mixed market into smaller groups of people whose needs are similar enough to serve the same way. The encyclopedia entry defines it as "the process of dividing mass markets into groups with similar needs and wants." The point is leverage — a message and an offering tuned to one coherent group will almost always beat a bland one aimed at everybody.

Marketers segment along four common bases (dimensions you can group people by), and most real segmentations combine several. Before the list, the useful intuition is that the first two describe who and where people are, while the second two describe how they think and behave — and the behavioral ones usually predict buying best.

  • Demographic — measurable traits like age, income, gender, education, or family status. Easy to find data on, but a weak predictor of what someone actually wants.
  • Geographic — where people live: country, region, city, climate, urban versus rural.
  • Psychographic — lifestyle, values, attitudes, and personality. Harder to measure, but closer to motivation.
  • Behavioral — what people actually do: how often they buy, what they use the product for, how loyal they are. Often the most actionable basis.

A segment is only useful if you can reach it, it is big enough to be worth serving, and the people inside it really do behave alike. A "segment" you cannot identify or reach in the real world is just a description, not a target.

Targeting: choosing where to compete

Once the market is divided, targeting is the choice of which segment or segments to actually pursue — a resource-allocation decision as much as a marketing one. The encyclopedia entry on the target market lays out four strategies that range from "one message for everyone" to "personalized for each individual." Before the list, the trade-off to hold in mind is reach versus fit: the broader your aim, the cheaper per person but the blunter the message; the narrower your aim, the sharper the message but the smaller the audience.

  • Undifferentiated (mass) marketing — one offering and one message aimed at the whole market. Cheap to run, but rarely compelling to anyone in particular.
  • Differentiated marketing — distinct offerings and messages tailored to several segments at once. More reach with more relevance, at higher cost.
  • Concentrated (niche) marketing — focusing all resources on one segment. Powerful for small or specialized players who would be drowned out in the mass market.
  • Micromarketing — tailoring down to a local area or even the individual, which digital data has made practical. As the entry notes, "Digital communications have allowed marketers to segment markets at ever tighter levels - right down to the individual consumer."

For a PM, targeting is the discipline that keeps a roadmap honest: choosing a segment also means choosing what you will not build and who you will not chase. A product that tries to win every segment usually ends up positioned for none.

Positioning: owning space in the mind

Positioning is the third STP step and the most counterintuitive: it happens not in your product but in the customer's head. The idea was popularized by advertising executives Al Ries and Jack Trout in their 1969 work and 1981 book Positioning: The Battle for Your Mind, whose central claim is that markets are won by occupying a clear, simple perception that competitors cannot easily dislodge. The encyclopedia summary captures their framing of positioning as "an organized system for finding a window in the mind" — you are not describing your product so much as claiming a slot in a crowded, distracted mind.

A practical tool here is the perceptual map (a simple chart that plots competing brands on two axes a customer cares about, such as price versus quality, or simple versus powerful). Plotting the competition reveals gaps — places in customers' minds that no brand yet owns — and a good positioning aims for an open, defensible gap rather than fighting head-on where a rival is already entrenched. Ries and Trout's enduring lesson is one of subtraction: a brand that stands for one clear thing beats a brand that tries to stand for everything. This is why positioning and the value proposition are tightly linked — positioning is the value proposition made memorable and distinct.

The marketing mix: the 4 Ps

STP decides who you serve and how you want to be seen; the marketing mix is the set of levers you actually pull to deliver on that. The classic version is the 4 Ps, a classification introduced by E. Jerome McCarthy in his 1960 textbook Basic Marketing and later popularized worldwide by Philip Kotler. Product Marketing Alliance, a practitioner community, frames them as an integrated whole: "These four elements—product, price, place, and promotion—form the foundation of any successful marketing strategy and help you view your offering through your customer's eyes." The four levers are:

  • Product — what you offer: the features, quality, design, packaging, and the actual problem it solves.
  • Price — what you charge, and the strategy behind it: discounts, tiers, and how price signals quality.
  • Place — how the product reaches the customer: distribution channels, retail, online, availability.
  • Promotion — how you communicate it: advertising, public relations, sales, content. (Note that promotion is just one of the four Ps — the part people mistake for all of marketing.)

The value of the framework is that the four levers must be consistent with each other and with your positioning. A premium product sold at a budget price through a discount channel sends a confused signal; the 4 Ps are a checklist for catching that kind of incoherence before customers do.

Extending the mix: the 7 Ps and the 4 Cs

The 4 Ps were designed in 1960 for physical goods, and the field has since stretched the model in two directions worth knowing. The encyclopedia entry on the marketing mix defines the mix itself as "the set of marketing tools that the firm uses to pursue its marketing objectives in the target market" and traces both extensions.

The first extension, the 7 Ps, adds three levers that matter when you are selling a service rather than a boxed product — because with services the experience is the product. Those additions are People (the staff who deliver the service), Process (how the service is delivered, step by step), and Physical evidence (the tangible cues — a clean store, a polished app — that reassure a buyer who cannot inspect an intangible service in advance). For most software products, the 7 Ps frame fits better than the original 4.

The second extension, Robert Lauterborn's 4 Cs, is a reframe rather than an addition: it rewrites each P from the customer's point of view. Product becomes Customer needs, Price becomes Cost (the total cost to the customer, not just the sticker), Place becomes Convenience, and Promotion becomes Communication (a two-way conversation rather than a one-way broadcast). The 4 Cs are a useful corrective: they keep a team from optimizing levers in a vacuum and force the question "what does this look like from the buyer's side?"

The marketing funnel and AIDA

Choosing whom to serve and how to reach them still leaves a question: how does a stranger actually become a customer? The oldest answer is AIDA, which stands for Attention, Interest, Desire, and Action — a model the advertising pioneer Elias St. Elmo Lewis sketched in 1898 to describe the mental steps a buyer passes through. HubSpot, a marketing software company, contrasts it with newer journey maps: "AIDA is more linear. It focuses on psychological progression rather than just customer journey touchpoints." Corporate Finance Institute describes AIDA as identifying "the stages that an individual goes through during the process of purchasing a product or service," and notes that later variants add stages like confidence and satisfaction the original leaves out.

AIDA became the shape of the marketing funnel — wide at the top where many people first notice you, narrowing as interest and desire filter the crowd, down to the few who act. The encyclopedia entry on the purchase funnel credits William Townsend with joining AIDA to the funnel image in 1924, quoting his observation that the process runs "continually from the general to the specific... to lead a customer from Attention to Interest, and beyond." The training site MindTools frames AIDA as a working copywriting tool: "These are the four steps you need to take your audience through if you want them to buy your product or visit your website." The funnel below shows the shape.

The modern customer path: Kotler's 5 A's

The classic funnel has a known weakness: it ends at the purchase and treats every customer as a solo decision-maker working straight down the stages. Philip Kotler's 5 A's, introduced in his book Marketing 4.0, updates the model for a connected world where people check reviews and ask friends before and after they buy. The five stages are Aware, Appeal, Ask, Act, and Advocate. The careers site Indeed summarizes the payoff: mapping these stages "can help them design practices that make the customer's purchasing process smoother and more satisfying."

Two changes from AIDA are worth holding onto. First, the Ask stage makes the social step explicit — between being attracted and buying, modern customers actively research and consult others, so reviews, word of mouth, and community become marketing channels you must tend. Second, the path does not end at the sale: Advocate means a happy customer recommends you to the next stranger, looping the funnel's exit back to its entrance. That loop is why retention and customer experience are now treated as marketing concerns, not just support concerns — your existing customers feed the top of the funnel.

The product life cycle

One more foundational idea ties the others together over time: products, like the marketing around them, are not static. The product life cycle describes the stages a product passes through from launch to retirement, and the right marketing mix changes at each one. The encyclopedia entry lists four stages — introduction, growth, maturity, and decline — noting that "Product sales pass through distinct stages, each posing different challenges, opportunities, and problems to the seller."

The practical lesson is that the same lever pulls in different directions depending on the stage. In introduction, marketing spends heavily to build awareness for something nobody knows. In growth, the job shifts to differentiation as competitors arrive. In maturity, when growth flattens, the focus turns to defending share and squeezing efficiency. In decline, the question becomes whether to harvest, reposition, or retire. For a PM, the life cycle is a reminder that a marketing plan written at launch will be wrong within a year or two — the mix must be revisited as the product moves through its stages.

Debates and common pitfalls

The fundamentals are stable, but a few genuine debates run through the field and are worth flagging rather than papering over. The biggest is whether the firm-centric 4 Ps still fit a customer-empowered world: Lauterborn's 4 Cs were an explicit argument that the 4 Ps make marketers think from the inside out when they should think from the customer in — a critique the marketing-mix literature treats as an ongoing tension, not a settled question. Practitioners genuinely disagree on which frame to teach first.

The funnel draws a similar debate. The linear AIDA funnel is widely criticized for implying a tidy one-way trip that real, socially-connected buyers do not take — which is exactly the gap Kotler's looping 5 A's was built to close. Treat the funnel as a simplifying picture, not a literal map of any one customer's behavior.

Beyond the debates, three pitfalls recur for newcomers. The first is conflating marketing with promotion — remember promotion is one of four Ps, and arguably the last one you should decide. The second is segmenting on traits that are easy to measure (demographics) rather than traits that predict behavior, producing tidy charts that do not improve targeting. The third is writing a value proposition from the builder's pride rather than the customer's need — the most common reason a technically good product fails to find its market.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer is the base layer beneath the more specialized marketing topics; each of these is a natural next step once the fundamentals are in place.

  • Go-to-market strategy — how segmentation, targeting, positioning, and the mix come together into an actual launch plan for a specific product.
  • Pricing strategy — a deep dive on the "Price" lever: value-based pricing, tiers, and willingness-to-pay research.
  • Brand strategy and brand positioning — extending Ries and Trout's positioning into a durable brand identity over time.
  • Growth marketing and the AARRR funnel — the modern, metrics-driven cousin of AIDA (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue) used in product-led companies.
  • Customer research and segmentation analytics — the quantitative methods behind defining and validating real segments.
  • Product-market fit — the product-side counterpart to a strong value proposition: evidence that a target segment genuinely wants what you built.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_fundamentals-of-marketing.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
